summary: Monthly oral fisetin treatment improved metabolism and cognition in male mice but had negligible effects in females.

Monthly oral dasatinib plus quercetin treatment had minimal effects in male mice but was detrimental in females, increasing adiposity, elevating SASP-related markers, reducing energy metabolism, and worsening object-recognition memory.
Intermittent senolytic treatment with dasatinib plus quercetin improves physical function and increases lifespan in old mice.
Fisetin extends both median and maximum lifespan in progeroid and naturally aged mice when administered late in life.
Fisetin reduces senescence markers across multiple tissues and lowers SASP factors such as IL-6 and TNF-alpha in aged mice.
Senolytic interventions significantly reduced mortality in old mice exposed to a SARS-CoV-2-related mouse coronavirus.

Long-term weekly dasatinib plus quercetin treatment ameliorates age-dependent intervertebral disc degeneration in mice, with earlier or mid-life intervention being more effective than very late treatment.
Senolytic treatment with dasatinib plus quercetin sharply reduced hepatocellular carcinoma incidence in Sod1 knockout mice.
Senolytic treatment reduced senescence markers, SASP factors, inflammation, and necroptosis in the liver of Sod1 knockout mice.
Dasatinib plus quercetin causes a transient decrease in flu-specific CD8 T-cell infiltration during primary infection in aged mice.
Intermittent fisetin supplementation improves arterial function in old mice.
Fisetin decreases cellular senescence markers and SASP-associated inflammation in old mice.
Navitoclax treatment causes rapid trabecular bone loss in aged mice.
Navitoclax strongly impairs osteoprogenitor and osteoblast function, causing cytotoxicity, apoptosis, and failure of mineralized matrix production.
The senolytic cocktail dasatinib plus quercetin mildly exacerbated obesity- and age-dependent liver disease progression and tumorigenesis in mice.
Senolytic treatment with dasatinib and quercetin does not improve overall influenza responses in aged mice.
